fragments.content: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) is characterized by obsessions (recurrent, intrusive, unwanted thoughts, urges, or images causing anxiety/distress) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviors or mental acts performed to reduce anxiety or prevent feared outcomes). Common obsession themes: contamination, harm, symmetry, taboo thoughts. Common compulsions: washing, checking, ordering, counting, mental rituals. Compulsions are ego-dystonic (recognized as excessive/unreasonable but difficult to resist). Symptoms are time-consuming (>1 hour/day) or cause significant impairment. Lifetime prevalence ~2-3%. Pathophysiology involves cortico-striato-thalamo-cortical circuit dysfunction, serotonin dysregulation, and glutamate abnormalities.

First-line psychotherapy: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP), a form of CBT involving gradual exposure to anxiety-provoking stimuli while preventing compulsive responses. ERP shows durable benefits post-treatment. First-line pharmacotherapy: SSRIs at higher doses than for depression (e.g., fluoxetine 60-80mg, sertraline 150-200mg, fluvoxamine 200-300mg) with adequate trial duration (10-12 weeks). Clomipramine (TCA) is effective but limited by side effects. Augmentation strategies for partial response include low-dose antipsychotics (risperidone, aripiprazole). Treatment typically continues 1-2 years before considering tapering. Deep brain stimulation reserved for severe treatment-refractory cases.

DSM-5-TR OCD criteria: Presence of obsessions (recurrent/persistent intrusive thoughts/urges/images causing anxiety/distress, person attempts to ignore/suppress/neutralize them) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviors/mental acts performed in response to obsessions or rigid rules, aimed at preventing/reducing anxiety or preventing dreaded events but not realistically connected or clearly excessive). Obsessions/compulsions are time-consuming (>1 hour/day) or cause clinically significant distress/impairment. Not attributable to substance effects or medical condition. Not better explained by another mental disorder (GAD worry, body dysmorphic disorder, hoarding disorder, trichotillomania, excoriation). Specifier: with good/fair vs poor/absent insight; tic-related.
